name: Staged capacity expansion gating the eight-suite pilot before scaling to twelve suites
consequences: Adopting an eight-suite pilot reduces upfront capital expenditure and staffing burden, enabling controlled operability within the host environment. It provides a defensible path to validate data quality, throughput, and safety metrics before committing renovations and additional equipment. The downstream trade-off is slower data collection and potential delays in recruitment milestones and publication timelines.
options: Operate eight suites for the full project horizon and defer expansion until data quality, false alarm rates, and staffing workflows meet predefined criteria.
Expand immediately to twelve suites and implement modular safety and staffing controls to manage risk while accelerating data accumulation.
Adopt a phased ramp from eight to twelve suites with quarterly milestones that trigger expansion only after independent verification of reliability and throughput.
review: Core tension: capacity growth versus validation of data quality and safety; weakness: the options assume clear, timely verification metrics without addressing potential renovation supply-chain delays or schedule slippage.

name: Recruitment strategy for NREM parasomnia cohort and matched controls
consequences: Expanding recruitment channels and flexible eligibility can speed enrollment and improve population representativeness, but may increase heterogeneity and annotation burden. Conversely, tighter eligibility and multi-site expansion can improve data richness but risk slower enrollment and potential selection bias. Overall, recruitment strategy shapes the statistical power for Aim 2 and the generalizability of findings.
options: Broaden recruitment channels to regional sleep clinics and neurologists, and offer participant-friendly processes to accelerate enrollment.
Tighten eligibility to require higher event frequency and caregiver support to improve data richness.
Implement a multi-site recruitment framework across Bonn and partner centers to diversify samples while maintaining protocol consistency.
review: Core tension: speed versus data consistency and representativeness; weakness: the options assume recruitment expands smoothly across sites without fully addressing regulatory harmonization and consent flow across centers.

name: Data governance framework for synchronized multi-stream recordings and privacy
consequences: A rigorous governance framework reduces privacy risk and enables compliant data sharing, bolstering trust with participants and sponsors. It may, however, constrain data availability and slow collaboration due to de-identification and access-controls burden, increasing administrative overhead. The downstream effect is improved ethical standing and potential for controlled external validation, at the cost of timelier data release.
options: Enforce strict de-identification, access controls, and audit trails for all physiological data and sensor streams.
Create tiered data access with de-identified datasets available under data-use agreements while keeping video restricted.
Engage a data collaboration partner to provide secure compute environments that minimize data export risk.
review: Core tension: privacy versus openness and cross-site collaboration; weakness: options do not explicitly address consent complexity or cross-border data transfer risk.

name: Semi-automated event-triage development strategy
consequences: In-house semi-automation with clinician oversight preserves interpretability and workflow alignment, but requires sustained internal resources and may slow progress. External collaborations can accelerate validation but risk misalignment with local data practices; an open benchmarking approach enhances transparency but may raise privacy or data-use concerns.
options: Develop in-house semi-automation with clinician oversight and iterative annotation loops to preserve interpretability.
Collaborate with external researchers to co-develop and validate triage models using the collected dataset.
Publish an open benchmark dataset and invite community contributions to improve labeling and triage workflows.
review: Core tension: development speed versus clinical reliability; weakness: options do not specify how labeling quality and inter-rater variance will be managed across collaborations.

name: Funding strategy and governance for clinical-epidemiology scale
consequences: A diversified funding approach reduces reliance on a single source and supports longer-term goals, but industry partnerships may introduce publication or data-sharing constraints and perceived conflicts. Maintaining grant-based funding preserves independence but may slow expansion. A transparent gating process aligns milestones with financial reality.
options: Maintain primary support from DFG and University funds with a staged external review gate before major budget increments.
Negotiate a pharmaceutical partner with clearly defined publication terms and data-sharing restrictions.
Establish a contingency funding plan that reserves resources for unproductive admissions and regulatory delays and includes a fallback plan.
review: Core tension: external funding constraints versus maintaining academic independence; weakness: options may not fully anticipate harmonization across multiple sponsors or long-run sustainability.

name: Safety, ethics, and operational risk management for residential monitoring
consequences: A robust safety and ethics framework reduces risk of harm and privacy concerns, enabling smoother operation and trust with participants. However, rigorous processes can increase staffing costs and slow decision cycles. Proactive governance supports timely approvals for expansions and data-sharing while protecting participants.
options: Standardize safety protocols, maintain safe staffing ratios, and conduct regular safety drills across all active suites.
Establish an independent safety and ethics oversight body with quarterly risk audits and rapid corrective action protocols.
Incorporate privacy-by-design elements with restricted video retention, informed consent for ongoing monitoring, and ongoing ethics consultations with participant communities.
review: Core tension: privacy protection versus data access for research; weakness: options may not fully address re-consent needs for long-term data use and scalable community engagement.

name: Cohort Yield-Driven Recruitment Cadence
consequences: Direct effect: Aligns recruitment pace with observed event yield, improving efficiency of bed occupancy and screening operations. A downstream trade-off is potential delays to overall enrollment if yield fluctuates or if adaptive pacing lags behind project milestones.
options: Adopt phased recruitment windows matched to interim event yield signals, slowing intake during low-yield periods to preserve resources.
Accelerate outreach and screening during anticipated high-yield months while expanding inclusion criteria to broaden eligible participant pool.
Implement adaptive pre-screening using remote questionnaires with risk flags to pre-select likely event-producing candidates for in-person assessment.
review: Core tension: matching recruitment pace to uncertain event yield risks underutilization of suites; weakness: the options do not address potential bias or inequity in access when pacing slows admissions.

name: Flexible Night-Shift Coverage Optimization
consequences: Direct effect: Improves responsiveness to unexpected event spikes and maintains safety margins; downstream: may increase payroll and coordination costs, and complicate vendor management.
options: Cross-train staff across multiple night shifts and implement on-call standby to respond rapidly to unexpected event spikes while preserving safety margins.
Develop a data-informed staffing plan that allocates more personnel on nights with higher predicted event probability and shorter response times.
Pilot a shared-services agreement with a regional sleep clinic to provide overflow coverage during high-demand periods without compromising patient safety.
review: Core tension: staffing flexibility versus regulatory and scheduling complexity; weakness: the options overlook potential vendor credentialing issues or hospital approval processes for cross-coverage.

name: Tiered Sensor Deployment Protocols with Triggers
consequences: Direct effect: Improves data quality by targeting enhanced monitoring only when likely events occur; downstream: increases operational complexity and requires rigorous calibration to avoid missed events or unnecessary escalations.
options: Design a tiered sensor protocol that escalates from dry-electrode EEG and mattress sensors to full PSG only after automated flags indicate probable events.
Roll out scheduled escalation nights where a subset of participants receives enhanced recording at regular intervals to validate ongoing data quality.
Institute strict privacy-preserving defaults that anonymize video feeds by default while enabling rapid reconsent if event triggers justify review.
review: Core tension: escalating data collection may burden participants and trigger false positives; weakness: the options don't address calibration of automated flags or standardization across nights.

name: Independent Data Stewardship and Access Window
consequences: Direct effect: Strengthens privacy and data governance; downstream: may slow external collaborations and extend data-sharing timelines.
options: Create a dedicated data stewardship role to oversee de-identification, access controls, and audit trails before any external data release.
Establish an access window with staged approvals for external researchers, ensuring reproducibility while protecting participant privacy.
Negotiate data-use agreements that require transparency on data lineage and code sharing to enable reproducible science.
review: Core tension: privacy-preserving governance vs enabling collaboration; weakness: options miss explicit incident response planning for data breaches.

name: Modular Expansion Governance Tied to Data Quality Thresholds
consequences: Direct effect: Aligns expansion with data-quality milestones; downstream: could reduce agility and slow scaling if thresholds prove conservative.
options: Authorize staged expansion of suites contingent on achieving predefined data quality metrics and safety certifications.
Delay expansion until independent audits confirm stable inter-rater reliability, low false alarm rates, and consistent staffing coverage.
Institute a quarterly governance review with clear thresholds, triggers, and contingency plans for scale-up or pause.
review: Core tension: expansion governance may hamper agility; weakness: options ignore cost risk and budget flexibility under changing grant conditions.

name: Participant-Centric Retention and Safety Enhancements
consequences: Direct effect: Improves safety and participant comfort, aiding retention; downstream: increases administrative load and budget pressure for ongoing support services.
options: Enhance participant safety by implementing real-time welfare checks, clear exit procedures, and responsive on-site medical support.
Introduce flexible stay scheduling and recreational breaks to reduce participant burden and maintain engagement over long stays.
Offer non-coercive, milestone-based compensation adjustments tied to ongoing consent and comfort levels.
review: Core tension: retention hinges on safety vs study throughput; weakness: options do not address differential attrition across parasomnia subtypes or long-term follow-up.

name: Dynamic staffing model aligned with nightly event risk assessments
consequences: Shifts adapt to observed event risk, improving safety and data capture on higher-risk nights. A downstream effect is increased schedule volatility for staff and potential coverage gaps during unexpected surges, which requires a formal on-call protocol and burnout mitigation.
options: Staff nightly shifts based on real-time event risk forecasts, reassigning technicians from lower-need periods to critical nights.
Maintain fixed staffing with an on-call pool to cover peak nights, reducing fluctuation but increasing idle time and payroll complexity.
Cross-train technicians to fulfill data QA and event adjudication during low-activity periods to stabilize coverage.
review: Core tension centers on protecting participant safety and data yield while avoiding staff burnout from variable hours. A weakness the options miss is that even with on-call pools, unanticipated nights can strain coverage and degrade morale if incentives and fatigue management are not aligned.

name: Targeted PSG escalation scheduling linked to triage confidence
consequences: Shifts full PSG deployments to nights flagged as high-confidence events by the triage tools, boosting data richness where most informative. The trade-off is greater participant burden on specific nights and increased scheduling complexity, which may affect retention if not communicated clearly.
options: Schedule full PSG on nights when triage flags high confidence to maximize yield with minimal total nights.
Limit full PSG to predefined high-yield windows, regardless of triage flags, to simplify operations.
Invite participants to opt-in additional PSG nights when consented, enabling richer data without compromising baseline protocol.
review: Core tension is balancing data richness with participant burden and schedule complexity; a weakness is potential consent fatigue and scheduling delays if opt-in is not clearly managed.

name: De-identified data collaboration with external partners under strict governance
consequences: Expands comparative phenotyping and external validation opportunities, improving generalizability. Downstream, it increases governance overhead and requires robust consent, security, and data access monitoring.
options: Establish formal data-sharing agreements with regional hospitals to broaden datasets while maintaining privacy controls.
Create a unified internal data schema across streams before external sharing to minimize interoperability friction.
Withhold external data access until independent audits demonstrate robust consent and traceability.
review: Core tension is between collaborative validation and strict privacy; a weakness is that external sharing may be slow due to regulatory review timelines, potentially delaying publishable analyses.

name: Environmental design adaptation to optimize naturalistic sleep behavior
consequences: Adjusts room ambiance to reduce reactivity to observation, potentially improving ecological validity. Trade-off: safety features must remain accessible, and too-natural environments could complicate monitoring in emergencies.
options: Modify lighting and acoustic settings to promote naturalistic sleep while preserving essential sensor reliability.
Retain domestic aesthetics but ensure safety overrides and emergency protocols are clearly visible to staff.
Offer optional home-like design variants that participants can select to improve comfort without affecting core data capture.
review: Core tension pits ecological validity against rapid emergency response; a weakness is that design changes may alter arousal detection latency if sensor coverage or room acoustics influence signal quality.

name: Expanded recruitment funnel leveraging regional sleep clinics and partner networks
consequences: Accelerates enrollment and improves sample diversity, enabling earlier data maturation. Trade-off: broader recruitment intensifies screening workload and may widen heterogeneity, requiring more robust stratification.
options: Launch parallel referrals from regional clinics with standardized pre-screening to speed enrollment.
Centralize screening through a single portal to reduce variability and improve data consistency.
Provide partner sites with performance milestones tied to recruitment yield and retention to motivate participation.
review: Core tension is accelerating enrollment while preserving data quality and representativeness; a weakness is that performance-based incentives could drive premature enrollment or selective referrals if not carefully designed.

name: Iterative annotation workflow optimization with dual scorer calibration
consequences: Improves inter-rater reliability and reduces review rework, advancing Aim 2 phenotyping efficiency. Downstream, this increases time-to-clean data for early publications and requires ongoing calibration resources.
options: Diversify scorer assignments to prevent fatigue and improve adjudication reliability across nights.
Schedule regular calibration sessions to align scoring criteria and maintain consistency.
Maintain a human-in-the-loop triage with rule-based prompts that flag uncertain events for rapid adjudication.
review: Core tension is achieving high scoring reliability without delaying data availability; a weakness is that calibration activities may siphon time from ongoing data collection if not integrated efficiently.
